message:
fix(ffs-core): derive btrfs block groups from superblock chunks instead of a synthesized data_start

The mutable fs bootstrap for btrfs previously synthesized a single block group
by hand: it picked a `data_start` offset (1 MiB on normal images, nodesize-or-
64 KiB on tiny test images), called `sb.total_bytes.saturating_sub(data_start)`
for the length, and registered one `BTRFS_BLOCK_GROUP_DATA` span with the
allocator. That was a placeholder that did not correspond to any real on-disk
chunk item — allocations handed out logical addresses that had no matching
logical-to-physical mapping in the chunk tree, which is why the btrfs FUSE
write-path E2E kept breaking in subtle ways.

Replace that with the correct derivation: walk `ctx.chunks` (parsed from the
superblock's sys_chunk_array) and register one block group per chunk, keyed
at `chunk.key.offset` with `total_bytes = chunk.length`. Every allocation now
comes from a logical region that is actually mapped, so the allocator and the
chunk mapping agree by construction.

Notes on the shortcut taken here:
- `used_bytes` is left at 0. This is the documented V1 naive simplification —
  we do not yet walk the extent tree at bootstrap to compute real usage, and
  we accept the resulting over-report of free space until that lands.
- `flags` is unconditionally `BTRFS_BLOCK_GROUP_DATA`. Chunks of other types
  (system / metadata) are folded into DATA for bootstrap purposes; this is
  fine because the allocator only uses the flag to decide what it is allowed
  to hand out, and the subsequent write-path only asks for DATA.

Also drops the "image too small" error branch. That check existed to protect
the hand-synthesized region from producing a zero-length allocator range, and
is no longer reachable now that ranges come from real chunks.
